title: Hierarchical attention/KV reducer tree
hypothesis: The flat reducer frontier showed p8/l32 and p16/l64 are not feasible as single wide blocks, while p2/l16 is feasible. A registered binary tree over the partial dimension should expose the area, power, and timing cost of hierarchical reduction before adding lane-sharding for l32/l64.

direct_comparison.primary_question: What are the Nangate45 PPA and latency costs for p8 and p16 registered binary reducer trees at l16?
include: p8/l16 and p16/l16 registered binary tree wrappers
two utilization points matching the prior reducer frontier
boundary rows if either tree point fails physical implementation
follow_on_broad_sweep: compose l32 and l64 from repeated l16 tree shards if p8/l16 and p16/l16 are feasible
feed measured tree latency and PPA into the clustered Llama7B schedule model

expected_benefit: separates partial-count hierarchy cost from lane-width pin/routing pressure
provides a physically measured alternative to the failed flat p8 and p16 reducer points
creates a reusable tree primitive for later hierarchical producer/reducer composition
risks: parallel tree inputs increase macro pin count and may still fail placement
l16-only results do not yet prove l32/l64 composition feasibility
standalone tree PPA may shift after integration with NoC and memory hierarchy

required_source_work.objective: Add RTLGen attention_kv_reducer_tree operation, example/config/sweep artifacts, and local RTL equivalence tests.

required_evaluations.objective: Measure p8/l16 and p16/l16 registered attention KV reducer trees and accept timing/flow failures as boundary evidence.

expected_result.reason: Determine whether hierarchical p2-style tree reduction can replace failed flat p8/p16 reducer points.
